free_text: Excitation functions of deuteron induced reactions on
natXe: New data up to 20 MeV
natural xenon gas (~1 bar)
Five Al-windowed,
52 micron, cylindrical, stainless-steel
gas cells , filled with natural xenon
were stacked together and three irradiations
were performed.  A thin Ti (12 microns) foil was
inserted at back side of each cell.
Additionally, 103 micron Al energy absorbers were
inserted before the cells. The number of incident
particles was obtained from the charge collected in an
insulated target holder, as well as via the
natTi(d,x)48V monitor reaction. For all irradiations
low intensity beams (200-240 nA) were used to minimize
the effect of density reduction. The initial energy of
the extracted beam was determined with an accuracy of
+-0.3 MeV, calibrated by the time-of-flight method.
The preliminary intensity of the incident beam was
obtained from the charge collected in the Faraday cup
and adapted via the re-measured monitor reactions.
Chapter 4
The initial beam uncertainty was +-0.3 MeV.
The uncertainty of the beam energy throughout
the stack increases to 1.6 MeV at the last foil.
beam current (8%)
detector efficiency (5%)
nuclear decay and branching data (3%)
effective target thickness (5%)
statistical and peak fitting (1-7%),
The uncertainty of the cross-sections were
estimated taking the independent relative errors of the
linearly contributing components (12-20%).
The experimental data were compared to the
results of the theoretical model codes TENDL-2011,
EPIRE-D and ALICE-D.
The measured experimental data were compared to
those found in the literature.
Fitted excitation functions were used for
determination of integral physical yields.
